content.text: Function Definitions

A function definition bridges two worlds: the Python function that does the actual work, and the schema object the model reads to understand when and how to invoke it. Every tool the agent can use must have both — the definition that the model sees at inference time, and the implementation that runs when the model selects it.

attrs.alt: Python function to tool definition mapping
attrs.title: From Python function to tool definition dict

content.text: The definition and the implementation are separate concerns.

The definition is documentation for the model; the implementation is code for your application. Keep them in sync — any mismatch between the schema and the actual function signature will produce runtime errors that are hard to diagnose.
Anatomy of a Function Definition
A function definition is a plain Python
dict
with three required keys. Pass a list of these dicts as the
tools
parameter:
Key
Type
Role
name
str
Must match the Python function name you register in TOOL_REGISTRY
description
str
Read by the model to decide WHEN to call this tool
input_schema
dict
JSON Schema object; defines the parameters the model will populate
Defining Multiple Tools
Most agents expose several tools. Define each as its own dict, then pass the list to every API call. A helper function keeps the structure consistent:
Keeping each definition in its own named variable makes it easy to compose tool subsets for different agent modes — for example, a read-only mode that omits
cancel_order_tool
Writing the Implementation
The definition tells the model what to call; the implementation does the actual work. The function signature must match the schema exactly — parameter names, types, and defaults must align:
A
TOOL_REGISTRY
dict is the simplest dispatcher pattern: look up the function by name, call it with the model's input as keyword arguments, and return a JSON string. The return value is sent back to the model as a
tool_result
content block.
Completing the Agent Loop
With definitions and implementations in place, the agent loop becomes mechanical: call the API, collect
tool_use
blocks, execute each one, feed the results back:
The loop continues until
stop_reason
is
"end_turn"
, meaning the model has enough information to respond directly without calling another tool.
Scoping Function Definitions

Each function definition should have a single, clear responsibility. The model selects tools based on description matching — broad, multi-purpose tools are harder to select correctly than narrow, single-purpose ones:
One function definition, one action — avoid 'do_thing' catch-alls
If two tools serve the same goal (e.g. search and filter), give each a distinct description that explains when to prefer it
Avoid tool names that shadow Python built-ins (list, format, get)
If a tool mutates state, say so in the description: 'Use only after confirming with the user'
Keeping Definitions and Implementations in Sync
The most common source of tool-use bugs is a definition that does not match its implementation. A few practices prevent this:
Risk
Symptom
Prevention
Schema says required but function has a default
Model always provides the arg; function silently uses wrong value
Mark optional params as non-required in the schema
Schema type is string but function expects int
Model sends '5'; function raises TypeError
Match schema types to Python type annotations
Description says 'returns list' but function returns dict
Model misinterprets results
Update description when return shape changes
Tool renamed in schema but not in registry
dispatch() returns 'Unknown tool'
Change name in both places atomically
Auto-Generating Definitions from Signatures
For simple tools, you can auto-generate the definition from the Python function's type annotations. This ensures the schema stays in sync with the implementation as the function signature evolves:
Auto-generation works well for simple parameter types. For complex cases — enums, nested objects, rich descriptions — write the definition manually and treat the function signature as the reference.
Returning Errors from Implementations

When a tool call fails (item not found, permission denied, invalid input), return a structured error dict rather than raising an exception. The model reads the error and can explain it to the user or self-correct:
Return error dicts with at least 'error' (machine-readable code) and 'message' (human-readable text)
Include a 'hint' field when there is a likely cause the model can relay
Never raise unhandled exceptions from tool implementations — they terminate the agent loop
Use a consistent error envelope across all tools so the model learns the pattern
Versioning Function Definitions
When a tool's input shape changes, adding a version suffix avoids breaking existing agents while letting new agents use the improved schema:
This pattern is especially useful when deploying to production agents that run continuously — old agents keep working on
send_email_v1
while new agents pick up
send_email_v2
. Once all agents have migrated, remove the old definition from the tools list.
Testing Function Definitions
Function definitions are configuration, not just code. Unit tests catch schema inconsistencies before they cause silent failures in production:
These four checks catch the most common mistakes: missing keys, required params that are not in properties, empty descriptions, and arrays without item schemas. Run them as part of your CI pipeline.
Summary
A function definition is the model-readable counterpart of a Python function. It declares the
name
(used in
tool_use
blocks), the
description
(the model's selection signal), and the
input_schema
(parameter types and constraints). The matching Python implementation accepts the same parameters and returns a result that is serialised to JSON and fed back as a
tool_result
. Keeping definitions and implementations in sync — same names, types, and semantics — is the primary maintenance responsibility when working with function definitions.
